title: Eye on the storm: IAG takes bet on AI
full_text:
Insurance Australia Group is betting the future of insurance could include claims paid automatically after heavy rain or destructive winds are detected by computer models.
The insurer has backed AI-powered start-up Adaptive Insurance in the latest investments from its venture capital arm, Firemark Ventures, as it seeks an early foothold in technologies it believes could reshape the insurance industry.
Alongside Adaptive Insurance, which provides a trigger-based payout model based on how much rain might fall or how powerful winds might be, Firemark Ventures chief Scott Gunther said IAG was looking to give itself options for the future.
Mr Gunther said a key consideration was getting under the hood of investments. He said IAG looked for investments that “shape our thinking and our strategic direction”.
“We’ve got to balance the execution of today with what’s changing tomorrow,” Mr Gunther said.
“We want to understand future risks. We want to know what we might need to prepare and develop inside our organisation.” It comes as the broader insurance sector faces continued cost pressure, as well as concerns operators have increased premium prices beyond the point many can afford.
Many of IAG’s recent investments have been pitched at attacking costs or improving efficiency.
The latest tally of investments from Firemark Ventures also includes a stake in US-based company DexMat, which is developing carbon nanomaterials intended to replace copper or aluminium in AI infrastructure and data centres.
This investment is aimed at better understanding how new materials will influence energy systems and industrial infrastructure. In addition, IAG has picked up a stake in American autonomous trucking company Gatik, something the insurer said was aimed at tapping into the insurance market for self-driving vehicles.
IAG has set aside $170m for the Firemark Ventures portfolio, with not all the cash deployed.
Its investments are on average $1-3m, with 36 companies on its balance sheet since Firemarket Ventures was founded in 2016.
Mr Gunther said IAG didn’t look at the “financial materiality” of these investments, but noted the insurer did hold “financial discipline” when picking companies.
Despite this, several of IAG’s early investments have gone on to be winners for the insurer, including cybersecurity firm UpGuard.
Mr Gunther said UpGuard was also playing a key role in IAG’s cybersecurity insurance offering.
Other portfolio companies have gone on to list on the ASX or the Nasdaq. The Firemark Ventures boss said IAG’s investments didn’t attempt to outflank its rival insurers QBE, Suncorp or Allianz. Instead, Mr Gunther said it was a tilt at a “first-mover advantage”.
Among its other recent investments is Socotra, which offers cloud-based core insurance software.
IAG, led by Nick Hawkins, has been on a years-long project to replace much of its core software in a bid to centralise its claims handling and pricing platforms.
The insurer also took a stake in BlueQubit, a quantum computing company. IAG has hopes the firm may offer catastrophe modelling, pricing, or advanced analytics.
In 2023, IAG invested in Raven AI, a technology provider using AI to inspect vehicles.
Some of IAG’s more recent investments are already coming into play, with modular building firm Spacecube providing the insurer housing for its Victorian customers who were hit by bushfires at the start of the year.
Australia’s insurers are all expected to face a weak outlook for growth in the year ahead, as they prepare to publish their full-year earnings in August. In a note to clients, Macquarie analysts warned IAG was likely to reveal “flat” expectations for growth in the year ahead.
They said rival Suncorp will likely forecast “low-single digits”. Macquarie noted competition was tight across motor and home policies, while their respective Australian commercial lines faced “difficult pricing”. The analysts warned both IAG and Suncorp would “call out challenges with their core technology programs”.
